description: Per-carrier boilerplate phrases — two sources: (1) n-gram extraction across denial bodies finding phrases appearing in 2+ denials from same carrier; (2) hand-curated keyDenialLanguage arrays from each Gmail dump JSON. Curated is higher quality but limited to what I manually flagged while reading; n-gram is exhaustive but noisy.

sources: intake:5

strategies.carrierTactic: shifts the goalposts from 'damage' to 'unrepairability' — requires specific visual proof (creasing + granule loss in surrounding shingles)
strategies.rooferTactic: brittle test demonstration with before/after photos to prove irreparability
strategies.patentMapping: Nationwide's photographic test (creasing + granule loss in surrounding shingles) closely matches the imageFeaturesScanned in Accurence US10089396 and WO2018156846. The carrier is invoking the patent-documented inspection criteria.
strategies.source: Gmail: 065417-GQ — Cheng / Vienna VA

strategies.carrierTactic: Nationwide standard close-after-denial pattern. Specific denial rationale is in the attached PDF (not transcribed in this email). The 'as is part of our claims process' framing presents closing as routine, not adversarial.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: Nationwide denials always have the substantive content in the attached PDF — the email itself is a thin wrapper. Counter-letter requires homeowner to forward the PDF for analysis. Reach out to Ryan Graham specifically asking for the underlying inspection report + weather data sources.
strategies.patentMapping: Nationwide's claim handling automation. The denial PDF likely follows the same format as the Nationwide PL Property Full Denial captured earlier for KYM GULLY (also under Thomes Buchanan adjuster) — which used wear/tear/deterioration template citation to policy provisions.
strategies.source: Gmail: Nationwide Claim 000883-GR — Kevin Melton — Full Denial

strategies.rooferTactic: Carlos's appeal letter shows the classic 'good standing member' + 'request reinspection with different adjuster' play. Counter is to deploy iTel report + Repair Agreement to force a second look.
strategies.patentMapping: Allstate's 'Replacement Cost' framing matches their patents US10685400 (mobile claims assessment), US10157445 (property image), US11257132 (surface dimension AI). The auto-issued estimate is patent-driven — the supplement request is how you re-engage human review.
strategies.source: Gmail: Allstate claim 000758479307 — Carlos Alfaro

strategies.carrierTactic: DUAL-CLAIM SHELL GAME — When supplement is requested under original claim, redirect to new claim. When supplement is requested under new claim, redirect back to original. Adjuster acknowledges damage verbally but denies in writing. Cites internal leadership consultation as cover.
strategies.rooferTactic: RECORDED VERBAL ACKNOWLEDGMENT is the strongest evidence — get adjuster on tape acknowledging damage source, then point to the written denial as contradiction. Plus the formal escalation template covers all bases: supervisor escalation, internal-staff inspection, policy language demand, regulatory complaint threat.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: This Luis Esteves escalation template is the GOLD STANDARD for Allstate denials. Counter-letter generator should produce ALL FOUR demands: (1) supervisor escalation, (2) staff adjuster reinspection (not 3rd-party), (3) specific policy language citation, (4) claim file documentation. Plus regulatory complaint threat (Virginia SCC or state equivalent).
strategies.patentMapping: Allstate's automated claim routing (US11798095, US11348134) creates the dual-claim ambiguity — different AI confidence scores per claim number create the runaround. Patent rules say 'route to highest-confidence outcome' but when multiple claims exist for same property, the system can't reconcile.
strategies.source: Gmail: Allstate Claim 0722264736 — Shaun Ali — Adjuster Runaround + Luis Esteves Escalation Template

strategies.carrierTactic: FOUR-PIVOT BAD-FAITH PATTERN: (1) Blame pre-policy hail, (2) Cite 2.25" magnitude that doesn't match photo evidence, (3) Once disproven, pivot to 'no hail >0.25" in 23 days', (4) Declare 'impasse' and direct to dispute resolution. The carrier never produces evidence for the 2.25" claim — it's a verbal report from seller laundered into an official-sounding denial basis.
strategies.rooferTactic: 5-source forensic storm report cross-referenced from federal sources (NOAA, SPC, NWS LSR, NEXRAD, CoCoRaHS). Hail size/damage chart photographic comparison. Visual contradiction of carrier's stated magnitude.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: When Allstate cites 'we have reached an impasse' or 'dispute resolution process' — that's the bad-faith escape hatch. Counter-letter should: (1) document each contradictory carrier position with timestamps, (2) demand specific evidence basis for each cited storm/magnitude, (3) cite VA Bureau of Insurance / State Corporation Commission complaint pathway, (4) request the carrier's automated decision rationale per emerging AI disclosure rules.
strategies.patentMapping: Allstate patents US11720971 (ML-based assessment), US11257132 (surface dimension AI), US11348134 (data processing ML) all support automated denial decisions. The pivoting suggests an AI confidence score that the human adjuster is back-rationalizing — when one rationale falls, another is substituted from the AI's ranked-reason list.
strategies.source: Gmail: Allstate Claim 0808617674 — Sue Baek — Carrier Pivoting Pattern (Pre-Policy → No-Hail → Impasse)

strategies.carrierTactic: Travelers' Brendon Myer follows the same template as Lauren Cook — minimal explanation in email, full detail in attached settlement letter + estimate PDF. Key levers: recoverable depreciation held back until invoice; contractor disputes must come BEFORE work starts (lock-in tactic).
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: Travelers Virginia-Carolina Claim Center uses identical template across adjusters (Lauren Cook + Brendon Myer + Christopher Clark + Matthew Birski + Adam Whitehead all sign with similar copy). The boilerplate detector should flag this template — when 5+ adjusters use identical phrasing, that's the AI/template fingerprint.
strategies.patentMapping: Travelers' standardized claim handling per their 238 filed patents (72 issued in last 5 years). The 'contact me before work is started' language is the supplement-gate — designed to keep all changes flowing through one channel.
strategies.source: Gmail: Travelers Claim I7M4883 — Jeanne Chang / Bang — Estimate + Depreciation Letter

strategies.carrierTactic: Travelers' Lauren Cook reclassifies contractor-documented damage as 'normal wear and tear' to dismiss roof replacement. Standard playbook — re-characterize hail damage as pre-existing condition. Travelers patents emphasize aerial imagery + drone-based damage assessment (top P&C AI patent holder).
strategies.rooferTactic: Homeowner-led reconsideration letter (more weight than contractor-led for PA-warning reasons). Emphasizes loyalty + dignity + 'detailed documentation' from contractor. Roof + siding both requested.
strategies.patentMapping: Travelers operates the largest insurance drone program (700+ FAA-certified pilots). Their 'wear and tear' classifier is the same engine documented in WO2018156846 (Dolphin AI) and US20190304026 (Betterview) which both define wear-and-tear visual signatures (granular wear contrast, uniform asphalt loss, edge curvature). Lauren Cook is invoking the AI-trained eye, not her own opinion.
strategies.source: Gmail: Travelers Claim I9G2378 — Abdul Deef Request for Reconsideration

strategies.carrierTactic: Travelers VA-Carolina Claim Center uses a standardized template across ~11 known adjusters. Wear-and-tear classification is the default for granule loss/blistering. ACV-first payment structure with depreciation held until completion. Hover.com aerial measurements as scope basis. Drone-based inspections (Travelers operates 700+ FAA-certified drone pilots).
strategies.rooferTactic: Know which manager to escalate to per adjuster (each has a documented manager email in their sig block). Use Chris Aycock's manufacturer-spec counter (see TravCo JEE8887 dump) for slope-coverage disputes — bypass damage classification, argue manufacturer installation requirements. For depreciation/ACV disputes, request the depreciation schedule basis.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: Travelers denial pattern: (1) Identify which Claim Center (VA-Carolina vs Mid-Atlantic vs other) — manager hierarchy differs. (2) Same template language across adjusters = strong boilerplate match signal even on new adjuster names. (3) For coverage scope disputes, prefer manufacturer-spec arguments over damage-classification challenges. (4) For payment disputes, ACV-first + depreciation withholding is standard — request line-item Xactimate + depreciation methodology basis.
strategies.patentMapping: Travelers is one of the top 3 P&C AI patent holders per Insurance Journal 2025 (238 patents filed Sept 2024, 72 issued in last 5 years). Their AI determination of roof condition from aerial imagery + 700+ FAA-certified drone pilots = patent-driven scoping. Per-adjuster phone routing keeps the same template language stable across the team — strong boilerplate-match opportunity.
strategies.source: Gmail: Travelers VA-Carolina + Mid-Atlantic Claim Center Adjuster Roster — captured from 4 threads

strategies.carrierTactic: Forces homeowner to choose contractor first BEFORE submitting estimates. Pricing tied to 'industry market standards' (typically meaning Xactimate baseline, not regional roofer pricing). Holds depreciation hostage until completion.
strategies.rooferTactic: Required: file supplement request via property-supplements.libertymutual.com. Resolution Specialist Brett Beatty (mobile 804-997-5698, FL Adjuster G043685) is the contact point. Must work directly through contractor.
strategies.patentMapping: Liberty Mutual is one of the 'big 3' carriers in AI patents per the Insurance Journal 2025 report. The 'industry market standards' language likely maps to their automated pricing engine — supplements require breaking the AI baseline with case-specific evidence.
strategies.source: Gmail: Liberty Mutual Claim 059304720-01 — Coverage Explanation & Settlement

strategies.carrierTactic: MIXED OUTCOME — Approve roof fully but EXCLUDE siding citing 'pre-existing damage prior to date of loss.' This is the partial-denial template Liberty Mutual uses to lower payouts. No specific evidence offered for the pre-existing claim. Plus mortgage-co payee adds friction to the homeowner's cash flow.
strategies.rooferTactic: Compare siding photos from claim submission against any prior available photos to dispute pre-existing claim. Request specific evidence basis for pre-existing determination. Often the carrier has no actual evidence — just declares it.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: Liberty Mutual 'no coverage for [component] due damage pre-existing': counter-letter must (1) request specific evidence basis for pre-existing determination, (2) ask for any prior inspections/photos referenced, (3) request the AI/algorithm methodology if vendor AI was used, (4) cite that absent evidence of when damage occurred, carrier must default to current claim DOL per most state contract-of-adhesion doctrine.
strategies.patentMapping: Liberty Mutual uses similar AI-based pre-existing damage scoring as Allstate (carrier-side patents) and vendor patents (US20190304026 Betterview, US20150228031 BuildFax roof-age modeling). The 'pre-existing' classification is the AI's pattern-recognition for what looks like older-vs-newer damage based on surrounding shingle condition.
strategies.source: Gmail: Liberty Mutual 061143908-01 — Neena Florio — Partial Denial on Siding (Pre-Existing)

strategies.carrierTactic: DOUBLE-INSPECTION pattern — carrier sends second inspector, frames findings as 'different perspective' to reinforce denial. Attributes granule loss to SOLAR PANEL INSTALLATION FOOTFALL (a novel deflection — blames the homeowner's contractor for the damage). References only the carrier's own weather history (8/20/2019 .75" hail), ignores any subsequent storms. Standard Liberty Mutual pattern: granule loss + soft-metals-test = wear and tear.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: For Liberty Mutual denials citing 'no pattern of damage to soft metals': counter with (1) photos of clear soft-metals impact, (2) third-party hail report covering ALL storms since policy inception (not just one cherry-picked event), (3) cite that 0.75" hail can absolutely damage shingles per NRCA standards, (4) request the inspection company's name + license + report under the right to coverage basis disclosure.
strategies.patentMapping: Liberty Mutual is one of the 'big 3' P&C carriers per Insurance Journal 2025 — their wear-and-tear classifier maps directly to WO2018156846 (Dolphin AI) and US20190304026 (Betterview) weather-damage criteria. The 'soft metals test' (looking for matching impact patterns on gutters/downspouts to corroborate roof hail) is the AI-trained inspector checklist.
strategies.source: Gmail: Liberty Mutual Claim 060253156-01 — Sarwar Siddiqui — Denied 2x for Wear & Tear

strategies.carrierTactic: TRIPLE-LAYER DENIAL — combine three theories without reconciling them: (1) no functional damage, (2) damage is pre-policy, (3) damage is only cosmetic. Plus mix multiple claim numbers into one denial letter. Engineer report basis without identifying methodology, engineer credentials, or weather data sources.
strategies.rooferTactic: TEMPLATE-QUALITY HOMEOWNER COUNTER — Six specific document requests: (1) full engineer report + attachments, (2) engineer name/company/credentials, (3) weather data + sources, (4) reconcile date inconsistencies, (5) claim-by-claim explanation, (6) why contractor findings rejected. Closes with formal-dispute language + records-preservation demand. This letter should be the canonical template for any State Farm engineer-report denial.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: When State Farm cites 'engineer report' with combined theories: counter-letter MUST request (a) engineer credentials, (b) methodology, (c) weather sources, (d) reconciliation of any date inconsistencies between denial letter and claim filing, (e) explanation for rejecting contractor findings. Records preservation demand should be standard.
strategies.patentMapping: State Farm patents US10565658 (Storm event roof damage detection), US20220414781 (Probable Roof Loss Confidence Scores), US10977734 (real property damage claims analysis) all support this layered-denial pattern. The 'engineer report' is the human cover for AI-generated probability scores below approval threshold.
strategies.source: Gmail: State Farm Claim 46-81Z7-05W — Nataly Danilova — Engineer Report Denial w/ Masterful Counter

strategies.carrierTactic: State Farm classifies the entire roof claim as 'No Hail' — the AI-driven binary determination from their patent US10565658 (Storm event roof damage detection) and US20220414781 (Probable Roof Loss Confidence Scores). When the AI's hail-confidence score doesn't cross threshold, the claim is automatically routed to a generic no-hail denial template.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: When a State Farm denial uses 'No Hail' framing, the counter-letter should: (1) cite specific hail evidence the AI scanner likely missed (test square hits per pattern in carrier-specific patent), (2) request the underlying confidence score under emerging AI disclosure rules, (3) reference neighbor approvals if available (forces adjuster to explain why same storm = different outcome).
strategies.patentMapping: State Farm patents US10565658 (Storm event roof damage detection) + US20220414781 (Probable Roof Loss Confidence Scores) drive this binary classification. The 'No Hail RFG Denial' template suggests AI confidence below threshold — the homeowner can rebut by showing patent-mapped evidence (creasing on surrounding shingles, soft metal damage, neighbor approvals).
strategies.source: Gmail: State Farm Claim 4673B816T — Brian Dolbeare — No Hail Roof Denial

strategies.carrierTactic: Standard State Farm template — denial substance in attached PDF, email body is thin wrapper. WCCS = Workforce Claims Customer Service deployed (catastrophe response unit).
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: State Farm partial denials via WCCS Deployed unit: request specific reinspection by NON-WCCS adjuster, ideally local field staff. Document inspector's behavior if rushed (timestamped photos, recording if state allows). Cite adjuster inadequate-investigation under VA/MD unfair claim settlement practices statutes.
strategies.patentMapping: State Farm patents US10565658, US20220414781 (roof loss confidence). The 'WCCS Deployed' designation = catastrophe-response team where AI scoring is more central. Rushed in-person inspection consistent with adjuster confirming the AI's pre-determined denial scope rather than independent assessment.
strategies.source: Gmail: State Farm 46-99F9-92W — Silvia Snyder — Partial Denial Letter

strategies.carrierTactic: SPLIT-CLASSIFICATION DENIAL — acknowledge hail damage to soft metals (which is hard to deny) BUT classify shingle damage as wear-and-tear (blistering + granule loss). Decline physical reinspection by citing 'sufficient photographs.' Push burden of proof onto homeowner at their own expense via engineer requirement. Cite 2-year suit-against-us provision early to discourage litigation.
strategies.rooferTactic: Homeowner cites NEIGHBORS APPROVED for same damage — pattern-of-approvals argument. Reese suggests filing under different DOL when first DOL is disputed. Internal Roof-ER suggestion: involve PA early for significant damage.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: Erie split-classification denials (soft metals YES / shingles NO): counter-letter should argue (1) pattern of soft-metal hail damage is impossible without corresponding shingle hits — physics-based argument, (2) neighbor approvals for same storm with identical damage type, (3) granule loss VS blistering distinction (granule loss with displaced asphalt vs thermal blistering pattern), (4) reinspection demand with internal staff adjuster — and if denied, file Virginia/MD state DOI complaint citing inadequate investigation.
strategies.patentMapping: Erie uses Verisk/CoreLogic vendor-based AI (Accurence US10089396, US10546264). The 'blistering + granule loss → wear and tear' classification is straight from the patent's documented decision rules. Vendor AI doesn't distinguish well between thermal blistering and hail-induced granule loss — they exploit this ambiguity in the AI's confidence threshold.
strategies.source: Gmail: Erie Claim A00007555686 — Hongyan Hang — Partial Denial w/ Reinspection Refusal

strategies.carrierTactic: Erie's standard settlement delivery — direct to homeowner. No adjuster name in this thread (would be on the PDF).
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: Erie thread is THIN — only the homeowner forward exists, the actual estimate is in the attached PDF. For full intel, the PDF needs OCR (which we now have via the transcribe endpoint). Future improvement: when a homeowner forwards a claim PDF, auto-transcribe and store.
strategies.patentMapping: Erie was not in the Insurance Journal top-3 patent holders. Likely uses Verisk/CoreLogic vendor patents (US10089396 Accurence, US20190304026 Betterview) rather than in-house ML. Erie tends to have shorter/simpler estimates — less AI-driven boilerplate, more human-adjuster-driven.
strategies.source: Gmail: Erie Insurance Claim A00006258004 — Dustin Tran — Settlement Estimate

strategies.carrierTactic: USAA's terse delivery model — minimal explanation. Estimate attached as PDF. Supplement requests via email reply only.
strategies.patentMapping: USAA patents US11410230 (aerial imagery → claim generation) and US11216889 (property items detection) drive this auto-estimate workflow. The lack of detailed reasoning in the email itself is consistent with patent-described automation.
strategies.source: Gmail: USAA claim 015826705-800 — Andre Jones

strategies.carrierTactic: BELOW-DEDUCTIBLE ESTIMATE — USAA writes the estimate just below the deductible amount so it 'technically' covers the loss but pays $0. The 'future claim denial' warning is meant to scare homeowners into not pursuing further documentation. iA = Initial AI cascade means the human adjuster's hands are tied by the vendor's algorithmic first decision.
strategies.rooferTactic: iTel sample for shingle availability test. Reinspection with new documentation. Pre-inspect heavy. Critical: get additional documentation BEFORE the initial AI fires the threshold-deny decision — once it's done, hard to flip.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: When facing USAA below-deductible denial: (1) iTel shingle availability test, (2) pre-inspect more thoroughly than initial AI scope, (3) document EVERY hail mark to push estimate above deductible, (4) recognize the iA cascade and request 'human-only review' explicitly under emerging AI disclosure rules, (5) request adjuster's photo report + AI confidence score per pending state regulations.
strategies.patentMapping: USAA patents US11410230 (aerial imagery → claim generation) and US11216889 (property items detection). The 'initial AI' (iA) the adjuster mentioned is likely Tractable AI (Verisk partner — see Verisk press release Mar 2023) which feeds USAA's claim ingestion. Vendor patent: US20190304026 (Betterview) wear-and-tear scoring drives whether the estimate crosses the deductible threshold.
strategies.source: Gmail: USAA Threshold Denial Pattern — 4 Threads — Atiphat / Pearson / Kinnick + earlier Jones

strategies.carrierTactic: USAA adjuster (James Robinson) deflects contractor advocacy by invoking unauthorized practice of public adjusting. Tactic shuts down further roofer-led arguments unless reframed strictly as contractor-technical-assessment.
strategies.rooferTactic: Reframe as CONTRACTOR'S TECHNICAL ASSESSMENT (building code + manufacturer warranty + material matching + industry standard). Explicit non-PA disclaimer. Decline-project leverage if partial approved. Joint inspection offer.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: When drafting counter-letters for USAA (and likely other carriers), AVOID: 'we respectfully request', 'negotiate', 'on behalf of', 'represent', 'advocate', 'matching requirements', 'state law requires'. USE: 'as the licensed contractor', 'technical/construction challenges', 'manufacturer warranty', 'code compliance', 'decline the project', 'joint inspection'.
strategies.patentMapping: USAA patents US11410230 + US11216889 are about automated claim generation from aerial imagery and property item detection. The PA pushback is a HUMAN escalation tactic — when AI auto-denials get challenged, carriers fall back on regulatory technicalities to limit contractor advocacy.
strategies.source: Gmail: USAA Claim 002384565-803 — Palmer / PA Warning thread

strategies.carrierTactic: ROOF APPROVAL + COSMETIC EXCLUSION — Approve the roof (where the AI's confidence is high) but write a SEPARATE cosmetic-damage-exclusion letter for gutters/downspouts. This separation creates two formal documents the carrier can defend independently if appealed.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: AMIG/Cincinnati Financial cosmetic-only denial on gutters/downspouts: counter-letter must (1) argue functional vs cosmetic distinction with specific evidence (dent depth, dimensional impact), (2) request the cosmetic exclusion policy language verbatim, (3) cite NRCA/IICRC guidelines that hail-damaged gutters lose drainage performance over time (functional, not cosmetic), (4) request the AI/vendor reviewer's identity and confidence threshold.
strategies.patentMapping: AMIG/Cincinnati uses vendor AI (likely Accurence US10089396 or Verisk Tractable). The 'cosmetic damage exclusion' is carrier-specific policy language that maps to vendor patent's exclusions field. The two-letter approach (estimate letter + cosmetic exclusion letter) is documented in patent claims handling workflows.
strategies.source: Gmail: American Modern (AMIG / Cincinnati) Claim 173980AB — Shawn Krause — Roof Approved + Cosmetic Gutter Denial

curatedPhrases.phrase: Based on the available weather data, the earliest hail event capable of producing the level of damage observed occurred in July 2025, which is prior to the policy inception date.
sources: intake:3

strategies.carrierTactic: ASYMMETRIC EVIDENCE BAD-FAITH PATTERN. Carrier (1) cites 'available weather data' to deny, (2) refuses to share or identify which sources, (3) deflects 3 separate explicit requests to share, (4) closes with 'Have a great day' to terminate. This is textbook bad faith — adjuster MUST be able to produce the underlying basis for a coverage decision per most states' unfair claim settlement practices statutes (e.g. Maryland § 27-303, Virginia § 38.2-510).
strategies.rooferTactic: Christian Bratton submitted 12 federal-source cross-referenced storm reports — well-documented. Now systematically requesting the underlying carrier source 4+ times to build the bad-faith record. Each ignored request becomes evidence. Next escalation: written formal demand for AI/automated decision system disclosure under emerging state regulations + DOI complaint.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: CRITICAL bad-faith signal: when carrier denial cites 'weather data' / 'available data' / 'reports' but refuses to identify or share sources after 2+ requests, this is patent-disclosed AI automation with no human-reviewable trail. Counter-letter should: (1) cite state-specific unfair claim settlement statute requiring disclosure of denial basis, (2) request the AI/automated decision system disclosure under emerging state regs (CA AB-3030, CO AI Act, etc.), (3) document each refused request with timestamps for DOI complaint, (4) escalate to supervisor/manager in writing — 'I am requesting in writing the specific weather data sources cited in the denial, and reserve all rights including filing a DOI complaint for failure to disclose the basis of the coverage decision.'
strategies.patentMapping: Allstate (Encompass's parent) operates under patents US11720971, US11798095, US11257132, US11348134 — ML-driven claim assessment. The 'we use a variety of weather reports' answer is consistent with an AI weather-correlation engine where the adjuster doesn't actually know which sources fired the denial. ShaJerrica may genuinely not have access to the underlying data — the model emitted a confidence score and template letter without surfacing sources.
strategies.source: Gmail: Encompass Claim 260257861 — Matt Foss — Bad-Faith Pattern (refused to share denial sources)

curatedPhrases.phrase: While it is possible that the exact siding may no longer be available Utica does not owe to match the existing siding

curatedPhrases.phrase: by replacing the entire damaged elevation to the corner trim leaking will not be an issue
sources: Gmail: Utica National Claim 0020059596 — Sandra Valenti — Elevation-Specific Approval + Matching Denial

strategies.carrierTactic: ELEVATION-SPECIFIC LIMITATION — Cover only the elevations the inspector noted hail on. EXPLICITLY refuse to match existing siding. Argue 'corner trim to corner trim replacement' is sufficient to prevent functional issues (leaking) — pushing back on matching requirements.
strategies.rooferTactic: MATCHING REQUIREMENT argument — Maryland, Virginia, and many states have matching requirements that contradict 'corner-to-corner' partial siding repair. Cite NRCA standards + state regs.
strategies.lessonForAnalyzer: Utica National elevation-specific partial: counter-letter MUST cite (1) state-specific matching requirements (VA § 38.2-510, MD Ins. Code § 27-303 unfair claim settlement practices, etc.), (2) NRCA standards on aged-vs-new material mismatch, (3) manufacturer warranty void if existing siding can't be matched, (4) cite that 'corner-to-corner' replacement creates visual mismatch that affects property value — request appraisal-based valuation of mismatch damage if matching denied.
strategies.patentMapping: Utica is a smaller regional carrier — likely uses Accurence (US10089396) for vendor AI scoping. The 'elevation-specific damage' approach is documented in Accurence patents as the cell-by-cell scoping logic.
strategies.source: Gmail: Utica National Claim 0020059596 — Sandra Valenti — Elevation-Specific Approval + Matching Denial
